query-title: Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956 – Adoption and Maintenance Provisions
query-text: The Act outlines the capacity of Hindu males and females to adopt, requiring sound mind, adulthood and, for women, unmarriage (or dissolution/death of husband). It sets conditions for a valid adoption, including the absence of a Hindu child of the same sex, a minimum age gap of 21 years between adopter and adoptee, and prohibition on adopting two children of the same sex simultaneously. The Act also presumes the authenticity of registered adoption documents. Maintenance provisions grant Hindu wives, widowed daughter‑in‑laws, children, and aged parents a right to be maintained, specifying grounds for separate maintenance and defining dependants. Keywords: adoption, maintenance, Hindu law, sound mind, age gap, desertion, cruelty, widowed daughter-in-law, dependants, registered document, Hindu wife, minor son, unmarried daughter Entities: Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956, Adoption, Maintenance, Hindu wife, Widowed daughter‑in‑law, Minor son, Unmarried daughter of pre‑deceased son

responses.responder: Capacity of Male Hindu to Adopt (Sec. 7)
responses.response-text: Capacity of Male Hindu to Adopt (Sec. 7): A male Hindu may adopt only if he is of sound mind and is not a minor.

responses.responder: Capacity of Female Hindu to Adopt (Sec. 8)
responses.response-text: Capacity of Female Hindu to Adopt (Sec. 8): A female Hindu may adopt only if she is of sound mind, not a minor, and either unmarried or her marriage has been dissolved, or her husband is dead.

Conditions for a Valid Adoption (Sec. 11): Adoption is invalid if the adopter already has a Hindu son (when adopting a son) or a Hindu daughter (when adopting a daughter); there must be at least a 21‑year age gap between adopter and adoptee if they are of opposite sexes; and two children of the same sex cannot be adopted simultaneously.
Presumption Regarding Registered Adoption Documents (Sec. 16): A registered document relating to an adoption is presumed to be authentic unless proved otherwise.

Maintenance of Wife (Sec. 18): A Hindu wife is entitled to lifetime maintenance from her husband; she may claim separate maintenance if the husband deserts her, is cruel, suffers from leprosy, has another living wife, keeps a concubine, or has ceased to be a Hindu.
Maintenance of Widowed Daughter‑in‑law (Sec. 19): A widowed daughter‑in‑law is entitled to maintenance from the estate of her deceased husband’s father, subject to the availability of assets.

responses.responder: Maintenance of Children and Aged Parents (Sec. 20)
responses.response-text: Maintenance of Children and Aged Parents (Sec. 20): Children and aged parents of a Hindu are entitled to maintenance from the person who would have been liable under the Hindu Succession rules.
Definition of Dependants (Sec. 21): Dependants include widows, minor sons, and unmarried daughters of a pre‑deceased son.

query-title: Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956
query-text: The Act defines a minor as any person who has not completed eighteen years of age. It establishes the father as the primary natural guardian of a Hindu minor, with the mother succeeding thereafter and serving as the ordinary custodian for children under five. The legislation delineates the powers of natural guardians, permitting them to act for the minor’s benefit but barring the mortgage, charge or alienation of immovable property without court approval. A testamentary guardian may be appointed to manage the minor's property through a will. Throughout all proceedings, the welfare of the minor is mandated as the paramount consideration. query-title: Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 – Key Provisions on Guardianship
query-text: The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 empowers courts to issue orders regarding the appointment and removal of guardians for minors. When deciding guardianship, the court must consider the minor's age, sex, religion, the proposed guardian's character and capacity, kinship proximity, the wishes of any deceased parent, and existing relationships, with the minor's welfare being paramount. Certain categories of persons are excluded from appointment as guardians, and the court may compel the return of a ward who leaves a guardian's custody. article-metadata.summary: The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 empowers courts to issue orders regarding the appointment and removal of guardians for minors. query-title: Family Courts Act, 1984 – Establishment, Jurisdiction and Procedural Framework
query-text: The Family Courts Act, 1984 empowers State Governments to constitute Family Courts for the expeditious disposal of family disputes. It delineates the courts' jurisdiction over matters such as nullity of marriage, restitution of conjugal rights, judicial separation, dissolution, maintenance, guardianship, custody, legitimacy and property disputes between spouses. The Act mandates that courts first attempt settlement through mediation before adjudication. Procedural matters are governed by the Civil Procedure Code with modifications, and the Act restricts legal representation, permitting parties to be assisted by a friend or social worker. query-title: Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961
query-text: The Dowry Prohibition Act, 1961 defines dowry as any property or valuable security given in connection with marriage and criminalises its giving, taking, or demanding. It prescribes imprisonment of up to five years and a fine for the giver or taker, and up to two years imprisonment with a fine for those who demand dowry. The Act bars advertisements for dowry, mandates that any dowry received is for the benefit of the wife or her heirs, and provides for the appointment of Dowry Prohibition Officers to enforce its provisions. It also references the related offence under Section 498A of the Indian Penal Code, which penalises cruelty linked to dowry demands. query-title: Key Provisions of the Indian Divorce Act, 1869
query-text: The Indian Divorce Act, 1869 governs divorce among Christians in India, outlining specific grounds for dissolution of marriage such as adultery, bigamy, and cruelty. It prescribes the procedural requirement that a petition for divorce be filed in a District Court or High Court. The Act provides for interim alimony (pendente lite) and permanent alimony, either as periodic payments or a lump sum. Custody, maintenance, and education of children are addressed under Section 41. An amendment introduced Section 10A, allowing divorce by mutual consent after a two‑year separation. query-title: Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act, 1937 – Scope and Key Provisions
query-text: The Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act, 1937 mandates that the Shariat governs all personal law matters for Muslims, superseding any contrary custom or usage. Section 2 specifically enumerates the subjects to which the Shariat applies, including intestate succession, dower (mehr), marriage, dissolution of marriage, maintenance, guardianship, gifts, trusts and wakfs. The Act operates alongside later statutes such as the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939 and the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019, which address grounds for divorce and criminalise instant triple talaq respectively. By codifying the application of Islamic law in these domains, the Act provides a statutory basis for the personal law of Muslims in India. Keywords: Shariat, Muslim Personal Law, Intestate succession, Mehr, Dower, Triple talaq, Dissolution of marriage, Guardianship, Wakf, Cognisable offence, Non‑bailable, Maintenance, Marriage, Custom override Entities: Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act, 1937, Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939, Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019, Shariat (Muslim Personal Law), Intestate succession, Mehr (Dower), Triple talaq (talaq‑e‑biddat), Wakf

responses.response-text: Application of Shariat (Section 2): Notwithstanding any custom or usage, the Shariat shall apply to Muslims in all questions relating to intestate succession, special property of females, marriage, dissolution of marriage, maintenance, dower, guardianship, gifts, trusts and trust properties, and wakfs.
Scope of Matters Covered: The Act expressly brings under Shariat law the core personal law domains, ensuring uniformity across Muslim communities and overriding conflicting local customs.

responses.responder: Relation to Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939
responses.response-text: Relation to Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939: While the 1937 Act sets the overarching applicability of Shariat, the 1939 Act enumerates specific grounds on which a Muslim wife may seek dissolution of marriage, such as the husband's disappearance, failure to maintain, imprisonment, impotence, insanity, disease, early marriage, and cruelty.

Mehr (Dower) Provisions: Mehr, an amount payable by the husband to the wife, may be prompt (mu’ajjal) or deferred (mu’wajjal). A wife may lawfully refuse cohabitation until the prompt portion of the mehr has been paid.

Interaction with Triple Talaq Legislation: The Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Marriage) Act, 2019 criminalises instant triple talaq (talaq‑e‑biddat) as a cognisable, non‑bailable offence, complementing the 1937 Act's emphasis on Shariat‑based marital governance.

query-title: Hindu Succession Act, 1956
query-text: The Hindu Succession Act, 1956 governs the devolution of property among Hindus. It establishes the hierarchy of heirs for male Hindus, designating Class I heirs (widow, mother, son, daughter) as primary successors, followed by Class II heirs, agnates and cognates. For female Hindus, the Act treats their property as absolute and provides that succession follows the heirs of their husband, then their own parents, and finally the heirs of the husband's father. The 2005 amendment made daughters coparceners by birth, granting them the same rights as sons in coparcenary property and allowing them to seek partition, thereby repealing the earlier restriction in Section 23. query-title: Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006
query-text: The Act defines a child as a male below 21 years of age and a female below 18 years of age. It declares child marriages voidable, permitting a child to obtain an annulment within two years of attaining majority. Courts are empowered to grant injunctions to prevent child marriages and to impose imprisonment of up to two years or a fine on adult males, officiants, or promoters who facilitate such marriages. Marriages undertaken by force or against the child's will are deemed void. Children born from void or voidable marriages are treated as legitimate. responses.response-text: Section 9 – Penalty for adult male marrying a child: Any adult male who marries a child is punishable with imprisonment for up to two years or a fine, or both.

query-title: Supreme Court Rules Divorced Muslim Women Entitled to Maintenance Under Section 125 CrPC
query-text: The Supreme Court held that a divorced Muslim woman is eligible to claim maintenance from her former husband under Section 125 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, despite personal law provisions that traditionally restrict such claims. The Court clarified that the right to maintenance is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution and cannot be denied on the basis of religion. It emphasized that the provision of Section 125 is a secular remedial measure applicable to all persons irrespective of personal law. The judgment sets a precedent for uniform application of maintenance rights across all communities and directs courts to enforce this right without prejudice. The decision also outlines procedural safeguards for filing and determining maintenance claims by divorced Muslim women. Keywords: Section 125 CrPC, maintenance, Divorced Muslim woman, Supreme Court, Personal law, Constitutional law, Article 21, Family law, Husband's liability, Gender justice, Legal uniformity, Secular provision, Right to life Entities: Supreme Court of India, Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, Section 125 CrPC, Article 21, Constitution of India, Muslim women, Divorced husband

responses.response-text: Background and Legal Issue: The case arose from a petition filed by a divorced Muslim woman seeking maintenance from her former husband. The contention was whether Section 125 CrPC, which provides for maintenance to wives, children, and parents, could be invoked by a Muslim woman after divorce, given the provisions of Muslim personal law.

Supreme Court Holding: The Court held that Section 125 CrPC is a secular provision that supersedes personal law where the right to life and dignity is concerned. Consequently, a divorced Muslim woman is entitled to claim maintenance under this section.

Rationale and Constitutional Basis: The judgment rested on the interpretation of Article 21, which guarantees the right to life and personal liberty, and the principle that the state must not discriminate on the basis of religion while enforcing statutory rights.

Procedural Implications: Courts are directed to accept maintenance applications from divorced Muslim women under Section 125 CrPC, assess the husband's liability based on his means, and award appropriate maintenance without requiring ancillary proof of dower (mahr) or other personal law concepts.

Impact and Future Outlook: The decision promotes uniformity in the application of maintenance law across religious communities and strengthens the protective ambit of the Constitution for vulnerable spouses.

query-title: Supreme Court: Marriage is pious; divorce on irretrievable breakdown not always desirable
query-text: The Supreme Court reiterated that marriage is a sacred institution in Indian society and cautioned against treating divorce on the ground of irretrievable breakdown as a routine remedy. While recognising genuine breakdown, the Court emphasized the need to balance individual rights with societal interest in preserving marriage. It directed courts to exercise discretion, consider alternatives such as reconciliation, and ensure that divorce does not become a preferred recourse. The judgment underscores the importance of the constitutional right to life and liberty under Article 21 while upholding family values. Keywords: marriage, divorce, irretrievable breakdown, Supreme Court, family law, Hindu Marriage Act, Article 21, reconciliation, legal discretion, societal values, pious institution, constitutional rights, family courts Entities: Supreme Court of India, Irretrievable breakdown of marriage, Marriage as a sacred institution, Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, Article 21 of the Constitution of India, Divorce

responses.response-text: Judgment Overview: The Court examined the growing trend of granting divorce on the ground of irretrievable breakdown and stressed that marriage, being a pious social contract, must not be hastily terminated.

Legal Reasoning: Balancing the right to personal liberty under Article 21 with the social objective of preserving family, the Court held that courts should assess each case meticulously, encouraging reconciliation where possible.

Implications for Family Courts: Family courts are directed to apply a discretionary approach, consider counselling, and ensure that divorce is not treated as a default remedy, thereby reinforcing the protective ethos of the Hindu Marriage Act.

query-title: Mukul Rohatgi urges Supreme Court to declare right to marry for LGBTQIA+ community
query-text: During the Supreme Court hearing on marriage equality, senior counsel Mukul Rohatgi contended that a clear constitutional declaration affirming the right of LGBTQIA+ individuals to marry is essential to eradicate stigma and provide legal recognition. He highlighted that while Section 377 has been struck down, the absence of a marriage right perpetuates discrimination. Rohatgi relied on Articles 14, 15(1)(a), 21 and 25 of the Indian Constitution, arguing that the right to marry is an integral facet of the right to life and personal liberty. He urged the Court to extend the constitutional safeguards to include same‑sex marriage, thereby aligning Indian law with international human‑rights standards. The argument underscores the need for affirmative jurisprudence to address societal prejudice and ensure equality before law. Keywords: marriage equality, LGBTQIA+, Supreme Court of India, constitutional right, Article 14, Article 21, Section 377, stigma, legal recognition, civil marriage, fundamental rights, non‑discrimination, personal liberty Entities: Mukul Rohatgi, Supreme Court of India, Indian Constitution, Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, LGBTQIA+ rights, Right to marry, Stigma

Arguments Advanced by Mukul Rohatgi: Rohatgi argued that denying marriage rights perpetuates social stigma and violates the equality clause (Article 14), non‑discrimination clause (Article 15(1)(a)), and the right to life and personal liberty (Article 21). He contended that marriage is a fundamental aspect of dignity and personal autonomy, and its denial is a form of discrimination prohibited by the Constitution.

Constitutional Provisions Cited: Articles 14, 15(1)(a), 21, 25 (freedom of conscience and religion) and 19(1)(g) (right to practice any profession, trade or business) were invoked to establish the legal basis for extending marriage rights to same‑sex couples.

responses.response-text: Potential Implications of a Positive Declaration: A judicial pronouncement affirming the right to marry would compel legislative amendment of personal laws, enable same‑sex couples to avail marriage-related benefits, and serve as a landmark step toward eradicating societal prejudice against the LGBTQIA+ community.

query-title: Justice Sanjiv Khanna Recuses from Supreme Court Review Petitions on Marriage Equality
query-text: The Supreme Court of India has listed review petitions challenging the landmark judgment that decriminalised consensual same‑sex relations and affirmed marriage equality. Justice Sanjiv Khanna, who was part of the bench that delivered the original judgment, withdrew from hearing the review petitions, citing the need to avoid any perception of bias. The remaining judges will continue the deliberations, with the petitions seeking to overturn the pronouncement that the Constitution guarantees the right to marry regardless of sexual orientation. The case underscores the evolving jurisprudence on equality, non‑discrimination, and fundamental rights under the Constitution. The recusal also raises procedural questions about bench composition and the handling of high‑profile constitutional review applications. query-title: Supreme Court Allows Video‑Conferencing Registration of Special Marriage Act Marriages
query-text: The Supreme Court of India, in response to the challenges posed by the COVID‑19 pandemic, ruled that marriages solemnised under the Special Marriage Act, 1954 may be registered through video‑conferencing. The judgment clarifies that the procedural requirement of physical presence can be satisfied by a live video link, provided the statutory conditions of notice, free consent and age are fulfilled. The order also directs the Registrar of Marriages to issue appropriate guidelines for technology standards, verification of identities and maintenance of records. This ruling expands access to marriage registration for parties residing in distant locations or under lockdown conditions, while preserving the statutory safeguards of the Act. Keywords: Special Marriage Act, video conferencing, marriage registration, Supreme Court judgment, COVID‑19, digital authentication, legal technology, inter‑state marriage, procedural safeguards, Registrar of Marriages Entities: Special Marriage Act, 1954, Supreme Court of India, Video Conferencing, Marriage Registration, Chief Justice of India, Registrar of Marriages, COVID‑19 Pandemic

responses.response-text: Background and Petition: Petitioners sought relief after the lockdown restricted physical attendance at the registrar’s office. They argued that the statutory mandate of physical presence violated the right to marry under Article 21 of the Constitution during the health emergency.

Supreme Court Judgment: The Court held that video conferencing can satisfy the requirement of physical presence under the Special Marriage Act, subject to verification of identity, free consent, and age. It emphasized that the technology must be secure, real‑time, and recorded.

Guidelines for Implementation: The Court directed the Central Government and the Registrar of Marriages to issue detailed procedural guidelines, including: (a) minimum technical specifications for video link; (b) authentication of parties through government‑issued IDs; (c) presence of at least two witnesses via video; and (d) preservation of the video record for a stipulated period.

Impact and Scope: The order expands accessibility of marriage registration, especially for inter‑state or inter‑regional couples, and sets a precedent for using technology in other statutory registration processes.

query-title: Supreme Court Clarifies Custody Under Section 167 CrPC Extends to All Investigating Agencies
query-text: The Supreme Court of India ruled that "custody" under Section 167 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) is not limited to police detention but also covers detention by any investigating agency, including the Enforcement Directorate (ED) and other central agencies. The judgment arose from a writ petition challenging the interpretation of custody in cases where non‑police agencies sought to remand a suspect. The Court held that the statutory language of Section 167 is agency‑neutral and that the same procedural safeguards applicable to police custody must apply to other agencies. Consequently, any extension of custody by these agencies must comply with the procedural requirements of Section 167, including judicial oversight and time limits. The ruling has significant implications for the conduct of investigations and the protection of individual liberty across multiple enforcement bodies. Keywords: Section 167, CrPC, custody, Supreme Court, Enforcement Directorate, investigating agencies, police custody, judicial custody, criminal procedure, rights of the accused Entities: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, Supreme Court of India, Enforcement Directorate (ED), Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI), Police custody, Judicial custody, Section 167 CrPC

responses.response-text: Section 167 of the CrPC – Custody Provision: Section 167 authorises the filing of a charge sheet and provides for the remand of an accused person in police or judicial custody while investigations are ongoing, subject to a maximum period of 60 or 90 days depending on the nature of the offence.

Supreme Court Decision: The Court clarified that the term "custody" is not confined to police detention; it includes any form of physical detention by any investigating agency, and the procedural safeguards of Section 167 apply uniformly.

Implications for Investigating Agencies: All central and state investigating agencies must now seek judicial approval before extending custody, adhere to the prescribed time limits, and ensure that the rights of the detained person are protected in the same manner as under police custody.

query-title: Supreme Court Bars Stays on Section 125 Maintenance Orders Except for Very Special Reasons
query-text: The Supreme Court has ruled that orders for maintenance under Section 125 of the Criminal Procedure Code cannot be stayed by appellate courts unless the applicant satisfies the stringent test of "very special reasons." The judgment emphasises the paramount importance of safeguarding the rights of women and children who depend on these orders for livelihood. While the Court recognised limited exceptions—such as evident abuse of process or manifest injustice—it cautioned lower courts against frivolous or routine stays. The decision is expected to streamline enforcement of maintenance orders and reduce procedural delays that often jeopardise the welfare of dependents. responses.responder: Principle of Non‑Stay for Maintenance Orders
responses.response-text: Principle of Non‑Stay for Maintenance Orders: The Court reaffirmed that Section 125 orders are meant to provide immediate financial relief and must not be subject to interim stays that could defeat their purpose.

Definition of "Very Special Reasons": Only when the applicant can demonstrate clear abuse of process, manifest injustice, or an irreparable harm unrelated to the maintenance claim may a stay be considered.
Impact on Women and Children: The judgment underscores the vulnerability of women and children dependent on maintenance, aligning with the constitutional guarantee of life and livelihood.

Procedural Guidelines for Appellate Courts: Lower courts are directed to scrutinise each stay application rigorously, record detailed reasons for any departure from the general rule, and prioritize speedy disposal.
Future Outlook: The decision is likely to influence subsequent case law, prompting a more consistent application of Section 125 across jurisdictions and reducing litigation delays.

query-title: Madras High Court: Marriage is Primarily for Procreation, Not Mere Carnal Pleasure
query-text: The Madras High Court observed that the essential purpose of marriage is procreation and the continuance of family lineage, rather than purely sexual gratification. In interpreting the Hindu Marriage Act, the court emphasized that the intention to have children is a substantive element of a valid marital contract. The judgment clarified that a marriage entered into solely for pleasure, without the intention of procreation, may be vulnerable to challenges under sections dealing with void or voidable marriages. This reasoning impacts divorce proceedings and the assessment of matrimonial disputes where the parties dispute the purpose of their union. query-title: Kerala HC Declares Marriage Does Not Bar POCSO Rape Prosecution of Minor
query-text: The Kerala High Court held that the fact a girl is married does not immunise the accused from prosecution under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act for rape. The court clarified that a minor, irrespective of marital status, cannot consent to sexual intercourse and any act of sexual penetration constitutes rape under Section 376 of the IPC and the relevant provisions of POCSO. The judgment reiterated that the marriage of a minor is void under the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006, and ordered protection measures, compensation and speedy trial. This ruling affirms that marital status is irrelevant when assessing offences against children and strengthens the legal safeguards for child victims of sexual violence. Keywords: Kerala High Court, POCSO Act, marital rape, child marriage, minor consent, rape prosecution, void marriage, criminal law, protection of children, IPC Section 376, legal remedy, compensation, protective custody, jurisprudence Entities: Kerala High Court, Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012, Indian Penal Code, Section 376, Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006, Minor, Marital Rape, Consent, Victim (minor girl), Accused

responses.response-text: Background: A minor girl, already married under a child marriage, was allegedly raped by the accused. The trial court had dismissed the POCSO complaint on the ground that the victim was married.
Legal Issue: Whether the marital status of a minor bars prosecution under the POCSO Act for rape and whether a child marriage renders the marriage void for the purpose of criminal law.

Judgment: The Kerala High Court ruled that marriage of a minor is prohibited and void, and the minor cannot give lawful consent. Consequently, the accused is liable under POCSO and IPC for rape, irrespective of the victim's marital status.
Directions and Relief: The court ordered protective custody for the victim, immediate compensation under the Victims of Crime Act, and directed the trial court to expedite the criminal proceedings.

Implications: The decision strengthens the legal position that child marriage does not provide a shield against sexual offences and reinforces the primacy of child protection statutes over matrimonial considerations.

query-title: Madras High Court Clarifies Date of Filing for Maintenance Applications
query-text: The Madras High Court, adjudicating a petition under Section 125 of the Criminal Procedure Code, held that the date of filing of a maintenance application is the date on which the petition is duly entered in the court's register, not the date of service of notice to the opposite party. The judgment elucidated that the filing date determines the commencement of the limitation period for filing a revision or appeal. It further observed that any delay in entering the petition in the register cannot be used to extend statutory time limits. The decision provides clear guidance to litigants and courts on procedural timelines in maintenance proceedings, ensuring uniformity in future applications. Keywords: Madras High Court, maintenance application, date of filing, Section 125 CrPC, limitation period, revision, appeal, court register, procedural law, family law Entities: Madras High Court, Section 125, Criminal Procedure Code (CrPC), Maintenance, Limitation period, Revision, Appeal

responses.response-text: Date of Filing Defined: The Court defined the date of filing as the date on which the petition is entered into the court's register after verification of the necessary documents, regardless of any subsequent administrative delays.

Impact on Limitation Period: The filing date triggers the limitation period for filing a revision or appeal. The Court emphasized that parties cannot claim an extended period based on the date of service of notice.

Procedural Requirements: The judgment reiterated the need for complete documentation and proper verification before entry into the register, as any deficiency may lead to rejection, but does not alter the filing date once entry is made.

Legal Reasoning: Relying on precedents and the language of Section 125 CrPC, the Court held that the purpose of the provision is to provide prompt relief to claimants, and thus the date of entry should be determinative.

query-title: Ashwini Kumar Upadhyay v. Union of India – Uniform Adoption and Guardianship Case Background
query-text: The case of Ashwini Kumar Upadhyay v. Union of India concerns the long‑standing demand for a Uniform Adoption and Guardianship Act to replace the fragmented personal‑law based adoption statutes in India. The petitioner argued that the existing framework, comprising the Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act, the Guardians and Wards Act, and the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, created disparities and impeded the best interests of children. The Supreme Court examined the constitutional validity of the proposed uniform legislation, its alignment with the right to equality, and the duty of the State to protect children. While the Court upheld the need for a uniform law, it directed the legislature to expedite the enactment of the Uniform Adoption and Guardianship Bill, emphasizing procedural safeguards and the paramountcy of child welfare. The judgment has prompted legislative and policy reforms, influencing adoption processes, inter‑religious adoption, and the recognition of guardianship across personal law communities. Keywords: Uniform Adoption and Guardianship, Supreme Court of India, Ashwini Kumar Upadhyay, Child welfare, Personal law, Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act, Guardians and Wards Act, Juvenile Justice Act, Equality clause, Inter‑religious adoption, CARA, Constitutional law, Legislative reform, Best interests of the child Entities: Ashwini Kumar Upadhyay, Union of India, Supreme Court of India, Uniform Adoption and Guardianship Bill, Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act, 1956, Guardians and Wards Act, 1890, Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, Central Adoption Resource Authority (CARA), Right to equality (Article 14, Constitution of India), Child's best interests

responses.response-text: Background: India's adoption law was fragmented across personal‑law statutes, leading to inconsistencies in adoption procedures for different religious communities. The petitioner highlighted the need for a single, secular law that would ensure uniformity, protect children's rights, and simplify the adoption process.

Issues Raised: Key issues included: (1) violation of Article 14 due to differential treatment; (2) lack of statutory provisions for inter‑religious adoption; (3) the inadequacy of guardianship provisions under the existing Acts; and (4) the necessity to align adoption law with international conventions such as the Hague Convention on Inter‑Country Adoption.

Supreme Court Findings: The Court recognized the legitimacy of the petitioner's concerns, affirmed that the State has a constitutional duty to protect children, and upheld the principle that a uniform law would advance the child's right to equality. It declined to strike down existing statutes but directed Parliament to enact the Uniform Adoption and Guardianship Bill without undue delay.

Impact and Legislative Response: Following the judgment, the Ministry of Women and Child Development accelerated drafting of the Uniform Adoption and Guardianship Bill. The proposed law seeks to replace the Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act, the Guardians and Wards Act, and the relevant provisions of the Juvenile Justice Act, introducing a single procedure for adoption and guardianship irrespective of religion.

Current Status: As of the latest updates, the Bill is pending parliamentary approval. Interim measures include procedural guidelines issued by CARA to streamline inter‑religious adoptions and safeguard the child's welfare pending the enactment of the uniform law.

query-title: Major General A.K. Kapur v. Union of India (Delhi High Court, 19 Feb 2009)
query-text: The petitioner, Major General A.K. Kapur, sought direction for his promotion to Lieutenant General after a Special Selection Board recommended him, but the Appointments Committee of the Cabinet withheld approval citing a vigilance‑related ban (DV Ban). The hold was based on a CBI FIR alleging possession of disproportionate assets, which the petitioner contested as unfounded. The Delhi High Court examined whether the ACC could lawfully withhold promotion on such grounds and emphasized that false or motivated allegations must not prejudice an officer’s unblemished record. Ultimately, the Court underscored the need for a balanced approach between accountability and protection of meritorious officers in matters of defence‑service promotions. Keywords: promotion, army, Special Selection Board, Appointments Committee of the Cabinet, Central Bureau of Investigation, FIR, DV Ban, vigilance, disproportionate assets, defence services, Delhi High Court, judicial review, merit, accountability Entities: Major General A.K. Kapur, Delhi High Court, Justice Sanjay Kishan Kaul, Justice Mool Chand Garg, Union of India, Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI), Appointments Committee of the Cabinet (ACC), Special Selection Board (SSB)

responses.response-text: Background: Petitioner joined the Army in 1971, rose to Major General, and was considered for promotion to Lieutenant General by a Special Selection Board on 5 Nov 2007.

CBI FIR and DV Ban: A CBI FIR dated 8 Oct 2007 alleged that the petitioner possessed assets disproportionate to his known sources of income. The ACC placed the promotion case in abeyance, treating it as a vigilance‑related ban (DV Ban).
Legal Issues: Whether the ACC could lawfully withhold promotion on the basis of an unproven FIR and whether the petitioner’s right to promotion could be overridden by mere suspicion.

Court Findings: The Court acknowledged the ACC’s discretion but warned that false or motivated allegations should not tarnish a clean service record. It highlighted the need for a balanced preliminary inquiry before registering an FIR against senior officers.
Conclusion: The judgment emphasizes protecting merit‑based promotions in the armed forces while ensuring vigilance, and calls for procedural fairness before imposing a DV Ban.

query-title: Citicorp Maruti Finance Ltd. v. S. Vijayalaxmi – Illegal repossession of a financed vehicle
query-text: The National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission held that a borrower who purchases a vehicle on loan remains the owner of the vehicle, even when the loan is secured by the vehicle. Consequently, a financier cannot take possession of the vehicle by force; repossession must follow due legal process. The Court distinguished a hire‑purchase agreement from a simple loan, emphasizing that the latter does not transfer ownership to the lender. It condemned harassment by agents of the financier and directed that any repossession must be in compliance with statutory protections. The judgment reinforced consumer rights and the rule of law in financial transactions involving motor vehicles. query-text: The petitioners, officers of the Public Health Department, filed a writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution seeking a mandamus to restrain the State Government of Andhra Pradesh from implementing a Central Government decision that allowed Assistant Surgeons from the erstwhile Hyderabad State to count their service before 1‑Nov‑1956 for promotion to Assistant Director of Public Health. The case centered on the interpretation of Sections 115 and 116 of the States Reorganisation Act, 1956, concerning the integration and seniority of service personnel after the formation of Andhra Pradesh. The Court examined whether the Central Government’s power to prescribe criteria for seniority lists was exhausted once those criteria were issued, and whether any further clarification required additional hearing. The decision addressed the balance of powers between the Centre and the State in matters of service integration following state reorganisation. responses.response-text: Petition and Relief Sought: The petitioners sought a writ of mandamus directing both the Central and State Governments not to prejudice their rights by counting pre‑1‑Nov‑1956 service for promotion purposes, and to restrain the State from implementing the Central decision on seniority integration.

Background: Reorganisation of Andhra Pradesh: Section 3 of the States Reorganisation Act added districts of the former Hyderabad State to Andhra, creating Andhra Pradesh on 1‑Nov‑1956, raising the question of how health service personnel from the two predecessor states would be integrated.

Statutory Framework: Sections 115 & 116: Section 115 mandates the Central Government to prepare criteria for inter‑state seniority lists, while Section 116 provides for the continuance of officials in their posts subject to conditions, forming the legal basis for the integration of health officers.

Scope of Central Government Power: The Court examined whether the Central Government’s authority ends once criteria are issued, or whether it retains jurisdiction to clarify and resolve disputes arising from the implementation of those criteria.

Continuous Service and Promotion Issue: The specific dispute concerned whether Assistant Surgeons from the former Hyderabad State could count service rendered before the reorganisation date for promotion to Assistant Director of Public Health in Andhra Pradesh.

Court’s Considerations and Outcome: The judgment analyzed the balance between Centre‑State powers under the Reorganisation Act, the need for procedural fairness, and the statutory intent to ensure equitable treatment of all affected personnel.

query-title: Ors v. State of West Bengal – Quashing of charge‑sheet under West Bengal Inland Fisheries Act, 1984
query-text: The petitioners, charged under Section 17A of the West Bengal Inland Fisheries Act, 1984 for allegedly filling up ponds, moved the Calcutta High Court under its inherent jurisdiction (Section 482 CrPC) to quash the charge‑sheet and the order of taking cognizance. They contended that the complaint was filed by a municipal executive officer rather than a fishery officer of the prescribed rank, that no notice was served as required by Section 17(10)(a), and that the ponds were not used for fishery, rendering the offence inapplicable. The State argued that the case diary showed actual pond filling and that the petitioners were also liable for conversion of land under the West Bengal Land Reforms Act. The Court examined the statutory requisites for taking cognizance, the procedural safeguards, and prior High Court precedents before deciding on the propriety of the charge‑sheet. query-title: Supreme Court Expands Scope of Article 142 in Mutual‑Consent Divorce Decrees
query-text: The Supreme Court, in a single‑bench decision, clarified that its power under Article 142 of the Constitution can be invoked to direct the execution of a mutual‑consent divorce decree even when the decree is passed after the statutory cooling‑off period. The Court held that the decree cannot be set aside on the ground of delay, provided the parties have complied with the procedural requirements of Section 13(1)(i)(a) of the Hindu Marriage Act. It further observed that lower courts must not entertain frivolous applications seeking rescission of a decree merely on the basis of procedural lapse. The judgment underscores the primacy of complete justice over technicalities in family law disputes and will streamline the finality of mutual‑consent divorces across the country. Keywords: mutual consent divorce, Article 142, Supreme Court, Hindu Marriage Act, Section 13(1)(i)(a), family law, decree execution, cooling off period, jurisdiction, legal precedent, complete justice, procedure vs substance, court orders Entities: Supreme Court of India, Article 142, Constitution of India, Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, Section 13(1)(i)(a), Mutual‑consent divorce, Family Courts, High Courts

responses.response-text: Background: Under Section 13(1)(i)(a) of the Hindu Marriage Act, a married couple may obtain a divorce by mutual consent after a mandatory six‑month cooling‑off period. Disputes frequently arise when the decree is executed after this period, leading to petitions for rescission.

Issue Before the Supreme Court: Whether a decree of divorce granted after the cooling‑off period can be set aside on the ground of procedural delay, and the extent of the Supreme Court’s power under Article 142 to compel execution of such a decree.

Supreme Court’s Observation Under Article 142: The Court held that Article 142 empowers it to pass any order necessary for doing complete justice, including directing the enforcement of a valid mutual‑consent divorce decree despite delay, provided statutory conditions are satisfied.

Impact on Lower Courts: Family Courts and High Courts must not entertain applications seeking to overturn a decree merely because of procedural lapse after the cooling‑off period; the decree stands unless substantive grounds of fraud or coercion are proved.

Practical Implications: The judgment streamlines the finality of mutual‑consent divorces, reduces litigation on technical grounds, and reinforces the principle that justice and finality outweigh procedural technicalities in family disputes.

query-title: Supreme Court Allows Single‑Bench Divorce Decree in Mutual‑Consent Cases under Article 142
query-text: The Supreme Court of India held that a decree of divorce under Section 13(1)(ii) of the Hindu Marriage Act can be passed by a single bench of the Court under the extraordinary powers of Article 142 of the Constitution. The decision clarifies that the Court may intervene where the Family Court’s procedural delay or a premature decree to a married woman causes hardship, without violating the principle of mutual consent. The judgment also underscores that such intervention does not disturb the legislative scheme, but merely ensures speedy justice. This precedent expands the scope of Article 142 in family law matters and provides guidance to lower courts on handling mutual‑consent divorce petitions. Legal Issue: Whether a single bench of the Supreme Court can exercise its extraordinary jurisdiction under Article 142 to pass a decree of divorce under Section 13(1)(ii).

Supreme Court Holding: The Court affirmed that it may, in exceptional circumstances, pass a divorce decree via a single bench under Article 142, provided the decision aligns with the statutory scheme and serves the interest of justice.

Implications for Family Law Practice: Lower courts must ensure procedural efficiency in mutual‑consent cases to avoid unnecessary Supreme Court intervention; parties can now seek relief directly from the apex Court where delay causes prejudice.

Conclusion: The judgment broadens the application of Article 142, reinforcing the Supreme Court’s role in delivering swift justice in family disputes without contravening the legislative intent of the Hindu Marriage Act.

query-title: Supreme Court allows divorced Muslim women to claim maintenance under Section 125 CrPC
query-text: The Supreme Court of India ruled that a divorced Muslim woman is entitled to claim maintenance from her former husband under Section 125 of the Criminal Procedure Code, despite the existence of separate personal law provisions. The judgment clarifies that the statutory right to maintenance is a secular remedy that cannot be denied on the ground of religion. The Court emphasized that the purpose of Section 125 is to prevent destitution and vagrancy, and it applies uniformly to all citizens irrespective of personal law. This decision bridges the gap between Muslim personal law and the general criminal law framework, ensuring gender‑neutral protection for divorced women. The ruling is expected to influence future cases involving maintenance under personal law disputes. Keywords: maintenance, alimony, divorce, Muslim women, Section 125 CrPC, Supreme Court, personal law, gender equality, civil remedy, Criminal Procedure Code, destitution, legal precedent, Indian family law, secular legislation Entities: Supreme Court of India, Section 125, Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, Maintenance (Alimony), Muslim Personal Law, Divorced Muslim woman (beneficiary), Husband (obligor)

responses.response-text: Background: Historically, divorced Muslim women could only claim maintenance under the provisions of Muslim personal law, which were often restrictive and varied across schools of thought. Many petitions alleged that this framework left them without adequate financial support.

Supreme Court Verdict: In a landmark ruling, the Court held that Section 125 CrPC provides a universal right to maintenance and is applicable to Muslim women as well. The decision dismissed the contention that personal law could bar the application of the secular statute.

Legal Reasoning: The Court observed that Section 125 was enacted to prevent vagrancy and destitution, objectives that transcend religious considerations. It reiterated that any law that curtails this constitutional guarantee would be unconstitutional.

Implications: The judgment ensures that divorced Muslim women can approach civil courts for maintenance, aligning their rights with those of women from other religions. It also sets a precedent for harmonising personal law with secular statutes in matters of social welfare.

query-title: Supreme Court Rules Child of Muslim Man and Hindu Wife Entitled to Father's Property
query-text: The Supreme Court held that a child born out of a marriage between a Muslim man and his Hindu wife is a legitimate heir and may claim a share in the father's property. The Court classified such a marriage as "fasid" – irregular but not void – distinguishing it from "sahih" (valid) and "batil" (void) marriages under Muslim law. Accordingly, offspring of a fasid marriage are considered legitimate, whereas only children born outside wedlock or from a batil marriage are illegitimate. The decision upheld a Kerala High Court judgment affirming the child's right to inheritance and clarified the impact of inter‑faith marriages on property succession under Muslim personal law. responses.response-text: Status of Children Born from Fasid Marriages: Children born of a fasid marriage are deemed legitimate and enjoy the same rights of inheritance as children from a sahih marriage. Only children born outside any marriage or from a batil marriage are considered illegitimate.

Property Rights of Legitimate Children: The legitimate child of a Muslim father, irrespective of the mother’s religion, is entitled to a share in the father's immovable property under the applicable personal law and succession rules.

Impact on Inter‑faith Marriages: While a marriage between a Muslim man and a Hindu (idol‑worshipper) is not sahih, it is not batil either; it is classified as fasid. Hence, the marriage does not become void, and the offspring retain inheritance rights.

query-title: Supreme Court: Exercise of Article 142 for Divorce on Irretrievable Breakdown Not a Straw‑Hat Formula
query-text: The Supreme Court held that it may invoke its inherent power under Article 142 of the Constitution to dissolve a marriage on the ground of ‘irretrievable breakdown’ even when one spouse opposes the relief, but such discretion must be exercised with great care and caution. The Court emphasized that marriage is a pious and spiritual institution in Indian society and warned against adopting a rigid formula for granting divorce under Article 142. Referring to the recent Constitution Bench judgment in Shilpa Shailesh v. Varun Sreenivasan, the Court reiterated that any exercise of this power must be guided by the principle of ‘complete justice’. In the specific case of an 89‑year‑old husband seeking divorce from his 82‑year‑old wife, the Court declined to grant relief, respecting the wife's sentiment of not wanting the stigma of being a divorcee. Judicial Caution and Care: Any exercise of Article 142 must be undertaken with ‘great care and caution’, balancing the equities of the parties and not reducing the discretion to a mechanical formula.

Marriage as a Pious Institution: The judgment reiterated the societal view of marriage as a sacred, spiritual union, emphasizing that social norms and emotional bonds should inform the Court’s approach to divorce.

Application in the 89‑Year‑Old Husband Case: Despite the husband’s petition for divorce on the ground of irretrievable breakdown, the Court denied relief because the wife, aged 82, wished to remain married and feared the stigma of being a divorcee.

query-title: Supreme Court Holds Marriage During Pendency of Belated Divorce Appeal Is Not Void
query-text: The Supreme Court ruled that a marriage entered into while an appeal against a divorce decree is pending is not void ab initio when the appeal is filed after the limitation period has expired. Referring to Sections 5, 11 and 15 of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, the Court observed that legislative intent does not deem a subsequent marriage void merely because a belated appeal is later filed. Section 15 bars remarriage only when an appeal is filed within the prescribed period or when the decree is stayed, neither of which applied here. Consequently, the Court allowed the maintenance petition and directed the husband to pay maintenance to the wife. Keywords: marriage, divorce decree, appeal, limitation period, Hindu Marriage Act, Section 15, void marriage, maintenance, Supreme Court, belated appeal, stay order, remarriage, legal intent, case law Entities: Supreme Court of India, Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, Justice Indira Banerjee, Justice M.R. Shah, Section 5 of the Hindu Marriage Act, Section 11 of the Hindu Marriage Act, Section 15 of the Hindu Marriage Act, limitation period for filing appeal, maintenance under the Hindu Marriage Act, void marriage

responses.response-text: Legal Issue: Whether a second marriage contracted during the pendency of an appeal against a divorce decree, filed after the limitation period for appeal, is void under the Hindu Marriage Act.

Court’s Observation on Legislative Intent: The Court held that it could not have been Parliament's intent to render a subsequently contracted marriage void merely because a belated appeal is later filed, especially when the appeal is beyond the limitation period.

Application of Section 15: Section 15 bars remarriage only when an appeal is filed within the prescribed limitation period, the decree is stayed, or the appeal is dismissed. In the present case, the appeal was filed almost a year after the limitation period, and no stay order existed, so Section 15 was not attracted.
Holding and Relief: The Supreme Court allowed the maintenance petition, directing the husband to pay maintenance to the wife, and affirmed that the second marriage is valid and not void.

query-title: Supreme Court Declines to Read Down Special Marriage Act for Same‑Sex Unions
query-text: On 17 October 2023 the Supreme Court Constitution Bench unanimously held that it could not strike down or read down the Special Marriage Act, 1954 to include non‑heterosexual unions. The Court reasoned that such a modification would involve complex workability issues, especially under Section 21A, and would constitute a legislative exercise reserved for Parliament. It also warned that voiding the Act for excluding queer couples would revert India to a pre‑independence regime of social inequality. The bench emphasized the institutional limitation of the judiciary in undertaking law‑making and reiterated that any amendment to the Act must be effected by the legislature. Keywords: Special Marriage Act, same‑sex marriage, reading down, judicial review, legislative competence, Article 14, Article 15, Section 4, Section 21A, Supreme Court, Chief Justice Chandrachud, queer marriage, Parliament, constitutional law, secular marriage framework Entities: Special Marriage Act, 1954, Supreme Court of India, Chief Justice D.Y. Narasimha, Justice Hima Kohli, Reading down (judicial interpretation), Legislative competence, Article 14 of the Constitution, Article 15 of the Constitution, Parliament of India

responses.response-text: Judgement Overview: The Constitution Bench, comprising Chief Justice Chandrachud and four other judges, unanimously refused to strike down or read down the Special Marriage Act to accommodate same‑sex marriages, stating that such a change lies within the legislative domain.

Complex Workability under Section 21A: The Court highlighted that Section 21A links the Act to personal and non‑personal succession laws, creating a ‘spider’s web’ of legislative inter‑dependencies that would make any amendment highly complex and potentially disruptive.

responses.responder: Historical Context and Pre‑Independence Era Concern
responses.response-text: Historical Context and Pre‑Independence Era Concern: Chief Justice Chandrachud warned that declaring the Act void for excluding queer couples would revert India to a pre‑independence scenario where inter‑religious and inter‑caste marriages were barred, undermining the secular purpose of the SMA.

Judicial Legislation vs Legislative Domain: The bench stressed that reading gender‑neutral words into the SMA would amount to judicial legislation, which is impermissible. The Court emphasized its institutional limitations and the need for Parliament to undertake any reform.

Constitutionality Debate: While Justice Kaul found the SMA unconstitutional for violating Articles 14 and 15, he concurred with the majority that the appropriate remedy lies with legislative amendment rather than a judicial strike‑down.

query-title: CJI Chandrachud Rejects Adoption Harm Argument in Marriage Equality Case
query-text: While hearing petitions seeking the extension of marriage rights to same‑sex couples, Chief Justice D.Y. Chandrachud observed that the claim that children raised by queer couples would suffer psychological harm is contradicted by the fact that Indian law already permits single individuals to adopt. Petitioners, led by Senior Advocate Dr. Abhishek Manu Singhvi, contended that marriage confers the right to adopt and urged the Court to read down the Special Marriage Act (SMA) to include same‑sex unions. The Court examined whether the SMA, enacted as a secular, agnostic legislation, can be interpreted to be inclusive of sexual orientation, invoking Articles 14, 19(1)(a) and 21 of the Constitution. The arguments emphasized equality, freedom of expression, and dignity, warning that a failure to recognise same‑sex marriage would create a discriminatory hierarchy inconsistent with constitutional guarantees. responses.response-text: Interpretation of the Special Marriage Act: Petitioners argued that the SMA, drafted as a secular statute to accommodate marriages not recognized by religion, should be read down to embrace same‑sex unions, contending that its purpose is agnostic to both faith and sexual orientation.

Constitutional Grounds Invoked: The submissions invoked Articles 14 (equality), 19(1)(a) (freedom of expression), and 21 (right to dignity) of the Constitution, asserting that denying marriage to queer couples creates an unconstitutional hierarchy and infringes on their liberty and dignity.

CJI’s Observations: Chief Justice Chandrachud emphasised that the law should evolve with societal changes, questioned the premise that children of same‑sex couples would develop a homosexual orientation, and asked whether the SMA can be interpreted as agnostic to sexual orientation without a ‘leap of faith.’

query-title: Supreme Court Directions on Queer Rights and Marriage Equality
query-text: In a split verdict delivered by CJI D.Y. Chandrachud and Justices Sanjay Kishan Kaul, S. Ravindra Bhat and P.S. Narasimha, the Supreme Court issued extensive directives to Union, State and UT governments to eliminate discrimination against the queer community in access to services, public perception and medical treatment. The Court mandated the cessation of conversion‑therapy, the establishment of safe‑house networks and hotlines, and the recognition of self‑identified gender without compulsory medical procedures. Specific police guidelines were laid down to prevent harassment of queer individuals and couples, including procedural safeguards before filing FIRs. The Court affirmed its jurisdiction under Article 32 and observed that marriage regulation lies with Parliament and state legislatures under Articles 245‑246, leaving space for future queer marriage legislation. responses.responder: Directions to Union, State and UT Governments
responses.response-text: Directions to Union, State and UT Governments: Governments must prevent discrimination based on gender identity or sexual orientation, ensure equal access to goods and services, sensitize the public, establish hotlines and safe‑house facilities, ban conversion‑therapy and non‑consensual medical procedures, and recognize self‑identified gender as male, female or third gender without compulsory treatment.

Police Guidelines: Police officers are prohibited from harassing queer individuals, must not force them to return to natal families, must protect them when complaints of family‑based violence arise, and must conduct a preliminary investigation under Lalita Kumari before registering FIRs in cases involving consensual queer relationships.

Mental Healthcare Act Modules: Under Section 29(1) the appropriate government must incorporate modules on queer mental health, and Section 29(2) programmes aimed at suicide prevention must specifically address issues faced by the queer community.

responses.responder: Conclusions on Jurisdiction and Marriage Regulation
responses.response-text: Conclusions on Jurisdiction and Marriage Regulation: The Court affirmed its authority under Article 32 to enforce fundamental rights, recognized queerness as a natural phenomenon, and clarified that marriage legislation falls within the competence of Parliament and state legislatures pursuant to Articles 245, 246 and Entry 5 of List III, leaving scope for future statutes recognising queer marriages.

query-title: Supreme Court Marriage Equality Case: Mukul Rohatgi Argues Declaration of Right to Marry for LGBTQIA+ Community
query-text: Petitions seeking legal recognition for same‑sex marriage were heard before a Constitution bench of the Supreme Court of India. Senior Advocate Mukul Rohatgi, appearing for the petitioners, sought a two‑fold relief: a declaration that marriage is a fundamental right for queer individuals under Articles 14, 19 and 21 of the Constitution, and that this right be recognised through an inclusive reading of the Special Marriage Act. He relied on precedents such as Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India, Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd) v. Union of India and Anuj Garg & Ors v. Hotel Association of India, and highlighted global developments to argue that state recognition would eliminate societal stigma. Rohatgi countered the Centre’s contention that the petitions represented a narrow, urban elite view, emphasizing that constitutional rights evolve with time and are not dependent on popular will. The arguments framed marriage equality as essential for the assimilation and equal treatment of the LGBTQIA+ community in India. query-title: Justice Sanjiv Khanna Recuses from Supreme Court Review of Same‑Sex Marriage Verdict

query-title: Supreme Court Hearing Highlights on Marriage Equality Petitions
query-text: The Supreme Court of India heard twenty petitions seeking recognition of queer marriages under the Special Marriage Act, Hindu Marriage Act and Foreign Marriage Act. The bench, led by Chief Justice DY Chandrachud, limited the scope to the Special Marriage Act, avoiding personal law matters. The Centre argued that marriage is a legislative matter and that the petitioners represent "urban elitist" views, but the Court rejected these contentions. The hearing also featured a debate on gender fluidity, with the Solicitor General asserting a biological definition of gender, which the Chief Justice challenged. A judgment is expected soon, carrying significant implications for LGBTQIA+ rights in India. Keywords: marriage equality, LGBTQIA+ rights, Special Marriage Act, Supreme Court of India, personal laws, gender fluidity, maintainability, Article 246, incremental change, urban elitist, sexual orientation, transgender rights, constitutional law, judicial review Entities: Special Marriage Act 1954, Hindu Marriage Act 1955, Foreign Marriage Act 1969, Supreme Court of India, Chief Justice DY Chandrachud, Justice Sanjay Kishan Kaul, Justice S Ravindra Bhat, Justice Hima Kohli, Justice PS Narasimha, Solicitor General Tushar Mehta, Senior Advocate KV Vishwanathan, Senior Advocate Jayna Kothari, Zainab Patel, Akkai Padmashali, Marriage Equality, LGBTQIA+ Rights, Gender Fluidity, Personal Laws, Maintainability

responses.responder: I. Centre Challenges Maintainability of the Petitions
responses.response-text: I. Centre Challenges Maintainability of the Petitions: The Union government argued that only the legislature can create or recognise marriage under Article 246 of the Constitution, contending that the Court lacks jurisdiction to grant marriage rights to same‑sex couples. The Chief Justice sought clarification on the petitioners’ canvas before addressing the maintainability issue.

responses.responder: II. Court Limits Challenge to Special Marriage Act
responses.response-text: II. Court Limits Challenge to Special Marriage Act: The bench announced it would confine its review to the Special Marriage Act, deliberately avoiding any interference with personal laws such as the Hindu Marriage Act. Both the Chief Justice and Justice Kaul advocated an incremental approach, focusing on a narrow statutory issue.

responses.responder: III. Centre Labels Petitioners as ‘Urban Elitists’; Court Rejects Claim
responses.response-text: III. Centre Labels Petitioners as ‘Urban Elitists’; Court Rejects Claim: In a counter‑affidavit, the Centre described the push for marriage equality as representing only urban elitist opinions and alleged that the popular will favours heterosexual marriage. The Supreme Court rejected this narrative, noting the absence of data and highlighting personal stories of transgender litigants to underscore the broader societal relevance.

IV. Debate on Gender Fluidity: Solicitor General Tushar Mehta maintained that the terms ‘man’ and ‘woman’ in the Special Marriage Act should be interpreted strictly on biological grounds. Chief Justice Chandrachud challenged this view, asserting that gender identity transcends mere biology, opening the discourse to a more expansive understanding of gender.

query-title: Supreme Court Rejects Marriage Equality and Adoption Rights for Queer Couples (Supriyo v. Union of India)
query-text: The Supreme Court, in a 3:2 majority judgment, declined to grant legal recognition to same‑sex marriages and also denied queer couples the right to adopt jointly. The majority, led by CJI D.Y. Chandrachud and Justice S.K. Kaul, held that CARA Regulation 5(3) violated Article 15 of the Constitution by discriminating against unmarried and queer couples. The dissenting judges upheld the regulation, arguing that the state must ensure stable homes for children and that the regulation was within statutory limits. The case, Supriyo v. Union of India (Writ Petition (Civil) No. 1011 of 2022), therefore leaves the status quo on marriage equality and adoption unchanged. Keywords: same‑sex marriage, adoption, CARA, Regulation 5(3), Article 15, discrimination, constitutional validity, majority judgment, dissenting opinion, family law, queer rights, marriage equality, Indian Constitution, stable home Entities: Supreme Court of India, C.J.I. responses.response-text: Marriage Equality Issue: The bench examined petitions seeking recognition of same‑sex marriages under Indian law. By a 3:2 majority, the Court held that the Constitution does not currently provide a right to marriage equality for queer couples, leaving the existing legal framework unchanged.

Adoption Rights for Queer Couples: The central question was whether CARA Regulation 5(3), which restricts adoption to couples with at least two years of stable marriage, violates Article 15. The majority found the regulation unconstitutional, allowing unmarried and queer couples to adopt jointly, while the minority upheld the regulation.

Majority Opinion (CJI Chandrachud & Justice Kaul): The majority concluded that CARA had exceeded its authority; the requirement of a two‑year marital relationship lacks a rational nexus with child welfare and constitutes indirect discrimination against atypical unions, violating Article 15.

Dissenting Opinion (Justices Bhat, Kohli, Narasimha): The dissent argued that the state, as parens patriae, must safeguard stable homes for children and that the regulation is a permissible classification aimed at achieving that objective; they held the regulation constitutional.

Legal Outcome: The judgment (2023 LiveLaw (SC) 900) results in a split verdict: marriage equality is not recognized, but the majority opinion opens the door for queer and unmarried couples to adopt, subject to future clarification.

query-title: Review Petition Challenging Supreme Court's Refusal to Legalise Same‑Sex Marriage
query-text: A review petition has been filed in the Supreme Court of India by four queer activists (Udit Sood, Saatvic, Lakshmi Manoharan and Gagandeep Paul) contesting the Court’s judgment in Supriyo v. Union of India, which declined to recognise same‑sex marriage. The petition argues that the judgment is self‑contradictory, unjust and constitutes an abdication of the Court’s duty to protect fundamental rights of LGBTQ+ individuals. It raises five specific grounds, chiefly the failure to interpret the Special Marriage Act, 1954 in a gender‑neutral manner and the refusal to acknowledge a constitutional right to a civil union for non‑heterosexual couples. The petitioners contend that the judgment violates Articles 14, 15 and 21 of the Constitution and breaches the principle of separation of powers. responses.response-text: Ground 1 – Interpretation of the Special Marriage Act, 1954: The petitioners claim the Supreme Court erred by refusing to read the Special Marriage Act in a gender‑neutral manner, thereby excluding same‑sex couples despite the Act’s purported purpose of allowing "any two persons" to marry. They rely on Justice S.K. Kaul’s dissent that the Act, if intended solely for inter‑faith marriage, cannot justify exclusion of non‑heterosexual partners and that the provision violates Article 14.

responses.responder: Ground 2 – Right to a Civil Union for Non‑Heterosexual Couples
responses.response-text: Ground 2 – Right to a Civil Union for Non‑Heterosexual Couples: Even if marriage is not extended, the petition argues that the Court should have recognised a fundamental right to a civil union, which could be operationalised by permitting registration of queer relationships under Sections 15 and 16 of the Special Marriage Act, thereby affording legal protection and benefits.

responses.responder: Ground 3 – Failure to Enforce Constitutional Equality Guarantees
responses.response-text: Ground 3 – Failure to Enforce Constitutional Equality Guarantees: The petition alleges the judgment ignored the Constitution’s guarantee of equality before the law (Article 14) and the right to life and personal liberty (Article 21), rendering the decision self‑contradictory and manifestly unjust.

responses.responder: Ground 4 – Abdication of Judicial Duty and Separation of Powers
responses.response-text: Ground 4 – Abdication of Judicial Duty and Separation of Powers: The review contends that by acknowledging discrimination yet refusing to intervene, the Court abdicated its constitutional obligation and misapplied the doctrine of separation of powers, effectively leaving the State unchecked in violating fundamental rights.

responses.responder: Ground 5 – Errors Apparent on the Face of the Record
responses.response-text: Ground 5 – Errors Apparent on the Face of the Record: The petitioners assert that the majority judgment contains clear factual and legal mistakes that are evident from the case record, warranting a reversal or amendment of the decision.

query-title: Allahabad HC Declares UP Conversion Act Does Not Bar Interfaith Marriages or Live‑In Relationships
query-text: The Allahabad High Court, in Noori & Another v. State of UP (Writ‑C No. 41127 of 2025), held that the Uttar Pradesh Prohibition of Unlawful Conversion of Religion Act, 2021 does not prohibit inter‑faith marriages nor prevent couples from cohabiting in live‑in relationships. The bench, headed by Justice Vivek Kumar Singh, clarified that an offence under Sections 3 and 5 of the Act requires a conversion achieved through misrepresentation, force, undue influence, coercion, allurement, fraud, or marriage, which is absent in consensual inter‑faith unions. The court granted mandamus directing police protection for the petitioners, emphasizing that the right to choose a partner, irrespective of religion, is protected under Article 21 of the Constitution. The judgment underscores the distinction between genuine conversion and personal choice in relationships, reinforcing individual liberty and personal autonomy. article-metadata.title: Allahabad HC Declares UP Conversion Act Does Not Bar Interfaith Marriages or Live‑In Relationships
article-metadata.summary: The Allahabad High Court, in Noori & Another v. State of UP (Writ‑C No. 41127 of 2025), held that the Uttar Pradesh Prohibition of Unlawful Conversion of Religion Act, 2021 does not prohibit inter‑faith marriages nor prevent couples from cohabiting in live‑in relationships. The bench, headed by Justice Vivek Kumar Singh, clarified that an offence under Sections 3 and 5 of the Act requires a conversion achieved through misrepresentation, force, undue influence, coercion, allurement, fraud, or marriage, which is absent in consensual inter‑faith unions. The court granted mandamus directing police protection for the petitioners, emphasizing that the right to choose a partner, irrespective of religion, is protected under Article 21 of the Constitution. The judgment underscores the distinction between genuine conversion and personal choice in relationships, reinforcing individual liberty and personal autonomy.

query-title: Assessing the Effectiveness of Tax Incentives for Foreign Direct Investment in the Global Minimum Tax Era
query-text: The article examines how tax incentives such as holidays, credits, and preferential rates are used by governments to attract foreign direct investment (FDI) and analyses their efficiency, equity, and neutrality. It argues that many incentives are redundant, offering little incremental investment while causing fiscal leakage and undermining public spending. The piece highlights equity concerns where large multinational enterprises receive preferential treatment compared to domestic SMEs, potentially fostering enclave economies and round‑tripping. Finally, it discusses how these incentives disturb market neutrality, distorting capital allocation and conflicting with emerging international standards like the global minimum tax. query-title: Supreme Court Holds Arrest Illegal Without Written Grounds Supplied Before Remand
query-text: The Supreme Court examined the constitutional mandate under Article 22(1) of the Constitution and Section 47 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, requiring that an arrestee be furnished the grounds of arrest in writing. While oral communication is permissible in exigent circumstances, the Court stipulated that written grounds must be supplied in a language understood by the arrestee at least two hours before the arrestee is produced before the magistrate for remand. Failure to meet this timeline renders both the arrest and subsequent remand illegal, entitling the person to release. The judgment also reinforced the duty to inform relatives or nominated persons under Section 48 of the BNSS 2023 and clarified that the procedural safeguard applies to all offences. The appeals were dismissed and interim bail in related matters was ordered to continue. Keywords: Arrest, Written grounds of arrest, Remand, Article 22(1), Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, Section 47, Section 48, Flagrante delicto, Procedural safeguard, Legal representation, Custodial rights, Supreme Court judgment, Criminal procedure, Magistrate Entities: Supreme Court of India, Bombay High Court, Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, Mihir Rajesh Shah, State of Maharashtra, Article 22(1) of the Constitution of India, Article 21 of the Constitution of India

responses.response-text: Constitutional and Statutory Requirement: Article 22(1) of the Constitution and Section 47 of BNSS 2023 mandate that an arrestee must be informed of the grounds of arrest in writing, in a language they understand.

Exception for Flagrante Delicto: In cases of immediate apprehension, oral communication at the time of arrest is allowed, provided written grounds are furnished within a reasonable time, and no later than two hours before the magistrate's remand hearing.

Consequences of Non‑Compliance: If written grounds are not supplied within the stipulated timeframe, the arrest and any subsequent remand are deemed illegal, obligating the release of the detained person.
Duty to Inform Relatives: Section 48 of BNSS 2023 requires police to inform the arrested person’s relatives or nominated persons about the arrest and place of detention.
Impact on Ongoing Proceedings: The Supreme Court dismissed the appeals, upheld interim bail in related matters, and directed that these principles govern future arrests to ensure uniformity.
